behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: This is primarily module handoff correctness (file discovery -> sort -> execution sequence). A higher System level was considered, but the behavior text focuses on ordering logic and does not require subprocess/OS/network-specific effects beyond local wiring.
behaviors.test_description: Run the runner against a local test DB with migration files intentionally out of lexical order (e.g., 001, 010, 002) and assert the resulting schema/data reflects execution in numeric-prefix ascending order.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration is necessary because unit-only sort tests miss discovery/execution wiring issues.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: Idempotency depends on interaction between migration execution and _migrations metadata persistence across runs. System was considered, but behavior text does not require real subprocess/network conditions; local DB + runner wiring is sufficient reality check.
behaviors.test_description: Execute runner twice on the same local test DB; verify second run skips already-recorded migrations and produces no duplicate side effects.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration is required to validate persisted state handoff between runs.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: Failure handling spans executor, transaction management, and run-control logic. System was considered, but behavior text does not require subprocess/OS-level behavior; a local real Postgres integration test can validate rollback + stop-on-failure round-trip.
behaviors.test_description: Create migrations where one file partially mutates data then errors; run against local Postgres and assert failed migration leaves no partial changes and later migrations are not recorded/applied.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration test must include real transaction behavior, not mocked DB.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: The behavior explicitly requires concurrent separate processes and externally visible exactly-once effects. This matches REAL-INFRA and SYSTEM TRIAD (round-trip side effect across process boundaries). Lower levels with in-process fakes can hide locking/race failures.
behaviors.test_description: Start two real runner processes simultaneously against the same Postgres instance; after completion, verify each migration effect appears exactly once and _migrations has one record per migration.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: System is necessary because correctness depends on real cross-process concurrency and DB locking behavior.

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the _migrations table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: This is initialization sequencing across local modules and DB state. System was considered, but the behavior text does not require subprocess/OS/network anomalies; local integration with real DB state checks is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Run against a fresh local DB with a migration that depends on _migrations existing; verify runner creates _migrations first and then applies migration successfully in the same run.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration is needed to validate startup ordering and DB side effects together.
